video_title: MacroVoices #535 Michael Every: NAFTA and NAPTHA – Warcraft & Fartcraft
gist: Set against an ongoing Strait of Hormuz blockade and shut-in of 13–15 mb/d of oil, Rabobank's Michael Every argues the Iran crisis is one front in a broader US pivot from economic policy to 'economic statecraft' — weaponizing Fed swap lines, stablecoins, the SPR and a possible North American energy bloc — while commodity analyst Rory Johnston explains why oil has stayed near $97–98 Brent (a ~50% collapse in Chinese crude imports masking record-pace inventory draws) and warns of a $150–200 spike once buffers exhaust.

themes.theme: Economic statecraft as the new policy regime

insights.claim: The US has shifted from 'economic policy' to 'economic statecraft,' openly using energy, swap lines, stablecoins and supply chains as tools of national strategy, which means an independent inflation-targeting Fed is logically incompatible with this regime.
insights.speaker: Michael Every, Rabobank global strategist
insights.so_what: Investors should expect policy (incl. Fed) to be subordinated to geopolitical/industrial goals rather than 2% CPI, reshaping how rates, FX and sector liquidity are set.
insights.quote: you cannot have an independent central bank which is trying to target 2% CPI ... and not have joined up thinking across every element of state and government towards the national security goals

insights.claim: Kevin Warsh's statement that Fed independence does not apply to dollar swap lines because they are a geopolitical transaction signals the Fed itself is moving into the economic-statecraft field, with swap lines becoming quid-pro-quo tools of the presidency/Treasury rather than neutral technocratic liquidity.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: Dollar liquidity access could become politically allocated; watch which non-traditional allies (e.g. UAE) get Fed backstops as a tell on US alignment.
insights.quote: he alluded to the fact that he thought Fed independence didn't apply to them because they were a geopolitical transaction

insights.claim: The Euro-dollar system gave the US power but de-industrialized it because global demand for dollars favors holding US financial assets over productive assets; this is a closed loop in which the dollar killed the industry that propped up the military that propped up the rules-based order.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: Frames the entire policy pivot: Washington wants to keep dollar dominance while reversing its de-industrializing side effects — bullish physical economy, ambivalent for pure financial-asset inflation.
insights.quote: the US dollar system killed the system the rules-based order which actually props up. So it's a closed loop

insights.claim: Stablecoins are intended to create lower US interest rates and suck Euro-dollars out of the global economy, replacing them with digital tokens in a 'buy-one-get-one-free' fiscal mechanism that also sorts allies from non-allies.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: Countries that accept US stablecoins, tariff China and split tech supply chains become the aligned bloc; stablecoin adoption becomes a geopolitical sorting signal and a source of US fiscal breathing room.
insights.quote: there's a sorting effect, there's a reorganizational effect, there's a buy one get one free and there's fiscal breeding room through all of that

insights.claim: Central banks as currently constituted are demand-management institutions that cannot handle structural supply-side shocks; the better tool for an energy shock is vast strategic reserves of products (as China uses), not raising or cutting aggregate borrowing costs with long lags.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: Implies policy will reach for physical interventions (SPR, export controls, swap lines) over rate moves; rate-cut/hike bets premised on classic Fed reaction functions may misfire in a supply shock.
insights.quote: central banks understand supply side or structural supply side shocks like a fish understands a bicycle

themes.theme: Fed under Warsh: balance sheet, supply-side QE, sector-specific rates

insights.claim: Warsh is publicly arguing AI will be very deflationary, creating room for rate cuts even as an oil-driven inflation shock looms — a contradictory backdrop that makes the rate path very hard to call.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: Rates could be cut into an energy spike on AI-deflation logic; positions assuming a clean hawkish response to oil inflation are at risk.

insights.claim: Warsh wants to reduce the Fed balance sheet at the same time Trump plans to expand the Pentagon budget by $500 billion next year — a circle that may be squared by redirecting QE-style balance-sheet support from financial assets to the physical economy.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: Suggests 'physical-economy QE' — liquidity steered to mines, ports, shipbuilding and defense supply chains — rather than broad asset-price support; a regime shift many market participants are not positioned for.
insights.quote: Walsh is openly talking about trying to reduce the Fed balance sheet ... at the same time Trump is talking about expanding the Pentagon budget next year by $500 billion

insights.claim: The Fed may move from one-size-fits-all policy to looking at which sectors of the economy need higher or lower rates, providing targeted balance-sheet support to strategic physical sectors rather than all favored financial sectors.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: Extra liquidity that flows to some sectors but not the usual favored ones overturns a 1–2 generation assumption of broad liquidity support; sector selection becomes paramount.
insights.quote: extra liquidity that goes to some sectors but it doesn't flow to all the favored ones which is what an entire generation if not two of financial market participants have grown up expecting as normal

insights.claim: The framing is 'World of Warcraft' financed by 'financial fartcraft' — using financialization to fund rearmament and re-industrialization, implying a structurally different liquidity paradigm.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: Captures the thesis in one image: liquidity directed at war-economy buildout (defense, mining, shipbuilding) over consumer/financial sectors.
insights.quote: using what I also call financial fart which is just financialization to try and prepare for a World of Warcraft

themes.theme: Iran/Hormuz scenarios and the NAPTHA energy-bloc thesis

insights.claim: The Strait of Hormuz remains effectively closed and Trump has floated lifting the US blockade of Iran 'as soon as Labor Day' (~95 days out, September), signaling a strategy of waiting Iran out rather than near-term resolution.
insights.speaker: host (Erik Townsend)
insights.so_what: A multi-month blockade horizon means inventory draws continue, raising the probability of a delayed but sharp oil spike into Q3.
insights.quote: the US blockade of Iran could be lifted as soon as Labor Day ... That's 95 days from now

insights.claim: Every floats a 'NAPTHA' scenario (North American Petroleum and Hydrocarbons Trading Hub Association): the US forms a closed energy loop with Mexico, Canada, Venezuela, Guyana, Gulf allies and key Asian refiners, fragmenting global energy markets so the bloc survives a crisis while everyone else faces much higher prices.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: A coercive energy bloc would entrench regional price divergence and reward North American/aligned producers and refiners while penalizing import-dependent regions — a structural trade in energy fragmentation.
insights.quote: instead of having NAFTA, you have NAPTHA, which is the North American Petroleum and Hydrocarbons Trading Hub Association

insights.claim: Canada's rhetoric flipped from PM Carney favoring a 'new world order' with China in January to seeking a 'new partnership with the US to make America great again' last week, illustrating how energy coercion can rapidly realign even daggers-drawn NATO allies.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: Watch allied rhetoric shifts as a real-time gauge of US statecraft leverage; realignment can happen fast under energy pressure.
insights.quote: from ... we're in favor of a new world order working with China ... to last week saying we want to form a new partnership with the US to make America great again

insights.claim: A US 'taco' (retreat from the Middle East, ceding Hormuz to an Iranian nexus) would be a '1956 Suez moment' that drops the US from great power to merely a power, shattering its global image and forcing a reassessment of US dollar and US-asset bullishness.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: A geostrategic retreat is a tail risk that would structurally undermine the dollar and US-asset premium even if the US remains a hyperpower.
insights.quote: I've referred to it as a 1956 crisis for the US in the way that the UK and France experienced one. And it dropped them from being great powers to just being powers

insights.claim: Resolution could ironically come from China working with the US against its own ally Iran, once Beijing concludes the damage to China from the crisis exceeds the value of keeping Iran as an ally.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: A China-US tacit deal against Iran is an under-priced bullish-for-resolution scenario; watch for rumors before mainstream reporting.

insights.claim: China was offered Fed-style backstop dynamics indirectly while the US extended swap lines to the UAE — a cash-rich, dollar-pegged, large-energy state that has now left OPEC/OPEC+ — signaling swap lines are being repurposed for new commodity-producing allies, not just the old Western/BOJ club.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: The UAE leaving OPEC+ plus a US swap line marks a realignment of energy and dollar plumbing; track which producers get backstops as the new alliance map.
insights.quote: swap lines ... have now been offered to the UAE which ... has a currency pegged to the dollar and has an awful lot of energy and is no longer in OPEC and OPEC plus

insights.claim: China stopped exporting refined products and fertilizer at the crisis onset, then resumed exports roughly an hour after the UAE left OPEC/OPEC+, suggesting China is reacting to shifting geopolitical 'tectonic plates' and positioning to offer product/RMB swap lines to stay ahead.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: China may emerge as a rival energy backstop via RMB swap lines; export-policy reversals are a tell on Beijing's strategic intent.

insights.claim: A closure of the Bab-el-Mandeb strait (Red Sea) — well within the capability of the Houthis and Iran's proxies — would be the big curveball that rapidly accelerates an oil spike, since oil blocked at Hormuz is currently pipelined to the Red Sea and exits there.
insights.speaker: host (Erik Townsend)
insights.so_what: Bab-el-Mandeb closure is a discrete catalyst to watch; it would remove the main workaround for Hormuz and spike prices quickly.

themes.theme: Oil market mechanics: why prices stayed near $97–98

insights.claim: 13–15 million barrels per day of oil production has been shut in for lack of export capacity, making this the core supply crisis; clearing stranded ships and restarting shut-in production after any deal will take weeks to months, not days.
insights.speaker: Rory Johnston, Commodity Context founder
insights.so_what: Even a peace deal does not flip supply back on quickly; the multi-month restart lag underpins a structural floor and delayed normalization.
insights.quote: this is the 13 to 15 million barrels of oil ... that has been shut in for lack of any export capacity

insights.claim: Iran releasing trapped tankers (out of ~2,000 vessels / ~800 large merchant ships in the Gulf) is effectively Iran tapping a strategic petroleum reserve, moving 'dead' produced oil into commercial inventory at a draw of 1–3 mb/d, but this does not fix the underlying shut-in production deficit.
insights.speaker: Rory Johnston
insights.so_what: Ship crossings are a finite buffer being drained, not a supply fix; once the trapped-oil stockpile depletes, the real deficit reasserts.
insights.quote: more ships means more SPR oil coming from one stock ... that's not accessible to the market to a broader stock that's accessible to the market

insights.claim: Chinese crude imports have collapsed roughly 50% — from ~12 mb/d seaborne pre-war to ~6 mb/d (heading lower in weekly data), which alone offsets about half the entire Hormuz supply shock and exceeds the combined SPR injections of every other country on earth.
insights.speaker: Rory Johnston
insights.so_what: China is now the single most important swing factor balancing the oil market; the durability of this import cut is the key variable for the price path.
insights.quote: Chinese crude oil imports have fallen by roughly 50% relative to prewar levels ... That's half of the entire supply shock from Hormuz

insights.claim: China's import collapse is not demand destruction (mobility, trucking, congestion and flight data show no collapse) and visible inventories actually rose then drew only modestly; the leading theory is China is drawing on invisible underground/non-satellite-visible inventories.
insights.speaker: Rory Johnston
insights.so_what: A huge piece of the supply-demand model is unobservable; once China's hidden buffers exhaust and imports rebound with Hormuz still closed, that's the trigger for the next leg higher.
insights.quote: you are seeing injections of up until now invisible inventories or nonvisible statistically inventories ... Underground inventories can't be seen

insights.claim: China's refining runs collapsed because pump-price caps held retail gasoline up only ~30% versus a global doubling, so feedstock costs exploded while product prices were capped, driving refining margins deeply negative — which also explains China's ban/limits on refined-product exports.
insights.speaker: Rory Johnston
insights.so_what: China's domestic price caps, not weak demand, drove the refining cut; this distorts global product flows and shields Chinese activity, masking the true tightness.
insights.quote: the cost of your feed stock has exploded while the cost of the product you sell has been capped. So your margins have imploded to deeply negative levels

insights.claim: The strong Chinese 'demand' seen pre-war (and back in 2023) was likely strategic stockpiling of refined products — possibly pre-positioned for a Taiwan contingency — not real demand, implying the pre-war global surplus was larger than appreciated.
insights.speaker: Rory Johnston
insights.so_what: Reassesses the global oil balance: apparent Chinese demand overstated true consumption, meaning structural demand is weaker and China is a more potent emergency backstop than OPEC.
insights.quote: some of the strong Chinese demand we were seeing likely wasn't real demand at all, but was rather stockpiling

insights.claim: Despite muted prices, oil inventories are being drawn down at the fastest pace of any crisis on record; in any other year/under any other president markets would already be far more panicked.
insights.speaker: Rory Johnston
insights.so_what: The calm price ($98 Brent high) masks record-pace depletion; the disconnect is the setup for a violent re-rating once buffers visibly hit bottoms.
insights.quote: We are now drawing that down at the fastest pace of any crisis on record

insights.claim: The historical link between inventory draws and futures-market panic has been 'neutered' by Trump's Truth Social posts, which can knock $20+ off crude in 10 minutes and scared speculators out — but physical rebalancing, unlike speculators, is immune to tweets.
insights.speaker: host (Erik Townsend) and Rory Johnston
insights.so_what: Tweet-driven suppression is a temporary cap; when physical traders (not speculators) drive the price, jawboning fails and the spike becomes 'unstoppable.'
insights.quote: all the truth social posts in the world won't make a bit of difference

insights.claim: Commodity markets balance supply and demand in real time and are not forward-looking, so prices won't spike until buffers are actually exhausted — mirroring early-COVID when crude rose for a month before the real-time crash.
insights.speaker: host (Erik Townsend)
insights.so_what: Don't expect oil to pre-price the shortage; the move comes only when physical balances break, likely end-June into July per Johnston.
insights.quote: they have to balance supply and demand in real time ... it wasn't until they actually cancelled all the flights ... that ... the crash was dramatic

themes.theme: Oil price forecasts and the spike trigger

insights.claim: Johnston sees ~$200 as the temporary demand-rationing level if Hormuz stays closed, not a new normal; he expects ~$150 if the blockade persists through Labor Day, and says current ~$97 pricing is 'still bullish' relative to fundamentals.
insights.speaker: Rory Johnston
insights.so_what: Asymmetric upside: a closed strait implies $150–200 spikes to ration demand; current prices under-price the tail.
insights.quote: 200 is essentially what I see as if we do not open hormos. That is the level that prices would need to rise to temporarily ration demand

insights.claim: Even the most bearish credible oil veterans (e.g. Ole Hansen sees a cap '~$200', Dr. Anas Alhajji argues only ~3–4 mb/d net deficit not 13, with oil below $100 in 2027 but possibly still rising in 2026) are not calling oil a short — no qualified physical-oil expert thinks it's 'no big deal.'
insights.speaker: host (Erik Townsend)
insights.so_what: The bull/bear range among experts runs from $150 to catastrophic; the consensus floor of credible views still points up, not down.

insights.claim: The key spike trigger is when Chinese imports stop falling and rebound (delta shifting from loosening to tightening) while Hormuz stays closed and EIA/high-frequency inventories rip below trailing seasonal bottoms — Johnston flags end-June into July.
insights.speaker: Rory Johnston
insights.so_what: Concrete watch-list: rising Chinese imports + sub-seasonal EIA inventories = the signal to position for the next leg higher in oil.
insights.quote: when the delta and the impulse shifts from loosening back to tightening. I think that's probably when we do that next rip higher

insights.claim: If the strait stays closed through Labor Day, hundreds of millions to a billion-plus barrels of additional inventory will be drawn, and even China's hidden 'black hole SPR' can't be big enough to prevent substantially higher prices.
insights.speaker: Rory Johnston
insights.so_what: Time does NOT favor price suppression; a long blockade makes a major spike close to inevitable on Johnston's framework.
insights.quote: if this is truly still going through the end of summer, then yeah, I I I don't see a way to avoid substantially higher prices

themes.theme: Inflation/deflation, gold, and the macro tail

insights.claim: Mike Green argues the oil crisis will be deflationary and force aggressive Fed cuts because while $150 oil is inflationary, $500 oil is deflationary (demand destruction) — a level Every calls 'a heart attack on the screen.'
insights.speaker: host (Erik Townsend), citing Mike Green
insights.so_what: A sufficiently large oil shock flips from inflationary to deflationary, scrambling the rate outlook; positioning for a clean inflation trade is risky at extreme oil levels.
insights.quote: Can't you see that $500 oil is deflationary?

insights.claim: Gold has fallen despite bombs falling — breaking the usual war/gold correlation — partly because Middle Eastern holders found gold hard to flee with: they sold gold for dollars to move capital abroad, and few want to re-buy heavy metal on the other side.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: A capital-flight mechanism (not just oil-inflation) explains gold weakness; the broken war/gold correlation may persist while the conflict drives dollar demand.
insights.quote: people were finding that they were having to sell gold for dollars to try and then move them abroad

insights.claim: Gold is in a four-month distributive consolidation after a parabolic blowoff at the start of the year, trading below its moving averages with the 200-day (~4415) as the key support; a break would likely accelerate the move lower, with no clear catalyst for a new bull leg until possibly Q3.
insights.speaker: host (Erik Townsend) and Patrick Ceresna
insights.so_what: Watch the ~4415 200-day on gold; macro headwinds (firmer dollar, rising rates) argue against gold longs near-term and for downside acceleration if support breaks.

insights.claim: High gasoline prices into the November midterms (5 months out) are a headwind for Republicans, but Every argues elections are binary and enthusiasm-driven, so a loss is 'not a gimme' given Democratic redistricting and the repeated humiliation of confident election forecasters.
insights.speaker: Michael Every
insights.so_what: Don't over-price a clean Republican midterm loss on gasoline alone; political outcomes (and thus policy continuity) are more uncertain than consensus assumes.

themes.theme: Market wrap: levels, FX, rates and the industrial-rebuild trade

insights.claim: The S&P 500 (~7553) just put in an eight-days-up, one-day-down rally — a magnitude seen only four times in ~20 years, ~20% trough-to-peak in two months — leaving it deeply overbought with poor asymmetry for pressing higher; hedging or reducing exposure is the logical move.
insights.speaker: host (Erik Townsend) and Patrick Ceresna
insights.so_what: A rare overbought condition argues for hedges/reduced risk; an oil break above $100 disrupting bonds is the candidate catalyst for profit-taking.
insights.quote: there's only four times we've seen a rally of this magnitude ... a very poor asymmetry at this moment for pressing the market higher

insights.claim: The DXY (~99.5) is attempting to break out of a one-month May range, with the euro at ~116 the key driver; deteriorating European conditions around next week's ECB meeting could weaken the euro and let the dollar rally, with further upside if Iran re-escalates to full-scale conflict.
insights.speaker: host (Erik Townsend) and Patrick Ceresna
insights.so_what: Watch the euro at 116 and the ECB; a euro breakdown or Iran escalation are the two paths to a dollar breakout.

insights.claim: The 10-year Treasury yield (~4.48%) sees the path of least resistance toward a 5% handle, as the oil-up/inflation-expectations-up/yields-up correlation of the last 2–3 months likely reasserts if oil turns higher; no bond-buying opportunity yet.
insights.speaker: host (Erik Townsend) and Patrick Ceresna
insights.so_what: Bias is toward higher yields with oil; a 5% 10-year is a realistic target and a potential equity-market stress catalyst.
insights.quote: can we see a 5% handle on the 10-year yield. It's certainly at this point ... the path of least resistance

insights.claim: Copper at 52-week highs is the most bullish chart in commodities, trading along its highs with dips bought and clear momentum, eyeing a run at the $7 level.
insights.speaker: Patrick Ceresna
insights.so_what: Copper momentum favors continuation toward $7; a relative-strength leader in the commodity complex.

insights.claim: Uranium hasn't participated in the broader rally and is seasonally weak until late August; a sharp Tuesday pop on the nuclear-fuels conference fully retraced, and a broad-market correction could drag uranium shares substantially lower without changing the intact long-term bull case (seasonal buying ~WNA conference Sept 7–11).
insights.speaker: host (Erik Townsend) and Patrick Ceresna
insights.so_what: Expect a uranium soft patch into late summer; long-term bulls may get a better entry before the September seasonal buying window.

insights.claim: Ceresna frames oil as an asymmetric long: hundreds of millions of barrels of depleted inventory put a structural floor under crude even with a peace deal, so the downside is definable/manageable while inventory depletion catching up to the market offers large upside.
insights.speaker: Patrick Ceresna
insights.so_what: The depleted-inventory floor makes long oil an asymmetric trade; even a peace deal leaves prices structurally elevated above pre-war levels.
insights.quote: You can't deplete hundreds of millions of barrels of oil inventory and then make a bet that oil's returning back to the pre-war levels

insights.claim: The trade of the week to express Every's industrial-rebuild thesis is long the PAVE ETF (~$57.24) paired with a July 17 2026 $55 protective put (~$1.25), effective cost ~$58.49, floor at $55, defining risk to ~$3.49/share (~6%) against a stretched, near-vertical equity rally.
insights.speaker: Patrick Ceresna
insights.so_what: A concrete, hedged way to play the physical-economy/economic-statecraft regime shift while protecting against a summer correction in an overbought market.
